---
title: "Theorem 2.7 — Conditional expectation as best predictor"
lean_name: "HansenEconometrics.best_predictor_rv"
chapter: 2
---

## Textbook statement

For a response $Y$ and conditioning variable $X$, Hansen's best-predictor
theorem says the conditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ minimizes mean
squared prediction error among square-integrable predictors that are functions
of $X$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[(Y-\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X])^2\right]
\le
\mathbb{E}\!\left[(Y-g(X))^2\right].
$$

In Lean, the candidate predictor is written as a function `g : Ω → ℝ` with the
assumption that it is
[$X$-measurable](../../assumptions/chapter2.qmd). The page uses the public
random-variable wrapper `best_predictor_rv`; the sigma-algebra theorem remains
available as backend infrastructure.

### Proof sketch

Write

$$
e = Y-\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X], \qquad d = \mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]-g.
$$

Then $Y-g=e+d$, so

$$
\mathbb{E}[(Y-g)^2]
=
\mathbb{E}[e^2] + \mathbb{E}[d^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[de].
$$

The cross term is zero because $d$ is $X$-measurable and the conditional
expectation error is orthogonal to every $X$-measurable square-integrable
function. The remaining term $\mathbb{E}[d^2]$ is nonnegative, so the
conditional expectation has weakly smaller mean squared error.

## Lean statement

The theorem lives in
[`HansenEconometrics/Chapter2CondExp.lean`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2CondExp.lean#L380):

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The public random-variable wrapper for Hansen Theorem 2.7.">theorem best_predictor_rv</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Response Y, competing predictor g, and conditioning variable X.">{Y g : Ω → ℝ} {X : Ω → β}</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="X must be measurable so conditioning on X means conditioning on the sigma-algebra generated by X.">(hX : Measurable X)</span>
    [SigmaFinite (μ.trim (conditioningSpace_le hX))]
    [IsFiniteMeasure μ]
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Both Y and the competing predictor g are square-integrable.">(hY : MemLp Y 2 μ) (hg : MemLp g 2 μ)</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The competing predictor is a function of X, expressed as measurability with respect to sigma(X).">(hgX : XMeasurable μ X g)</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The MSE of the conditional mean is weakly smaller than the MSE of any square-integrable X-measurable predictor.">∫ ω, (Y ω - condExpOn μ Y X ω) ^ 2 ∂μ ≤ ∫ ω, (Y ω - g ω) ^ 2 ∂μ</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

| Lean | Math | What it gives us |
|---|---|---|
| `{Y g : Ω → ℝ}` | $Y$, $g$ | **Given:** response and competing predictor. |
| `{X : Ω → β}` | $X$ | **Given:** conditioning variable. |
| `(hX : Measurable X)` | $X$ measurable | Needed to form the conditioning information $\sigma(X)$. |
| `[IsFiniteMeasure μ]` | finite sample measure | The $L^2$ projection theorem is used in a finite-measure setting. |
| `(hY : MemLp Y 2 μ)` | $Y \in L^2$ | Square-integrability of the response. |
| `(hg : MemLp g 2 μ)` | $g \in L^2$ | Square-integrability of the competing predictor. |
| `(hgX : XMeasurable μ X g)` | $g$ is $\sigma(X)$-measurable | The formal version of "predictors that are functions of $X$." |
| `∫ ... ≤ ∫ ...` | MSE inequality | The conditional expectation has weakly lower squared prediction error. |

## Translation notes

- `condExpOn μ Y X` is the public wrapper for $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$.
- `XMeasurable μ X g` is the Lean predicate saying that `g` is measurable
  with respect to the information generated by `X`.
- The backend theorem
  [`integral_sq_sub_condExp_le_integral_sq_sub_X`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2CondExp.lean#L232)
  proves the same inequality at the sigma-algebra layer. The public theorem
  just specializes it to conditioning variables.

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

The public theorem is intentionally thin: it translates the variable-facing
notation into the sigma-algebra theorem.

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="The public theorem statement: conditional expectation on X beats any square-integrable X-measurable predictor.">theorem best_predictor_rv
    {Y g : Ω → ℝ} {X : Ω → β}
    (hX : Measurable X)
    [SigmaFinite (μ.trim (conditioningSpace_le hX))]
    [IsFiniteMeasure μ]
    (hY : MemLp Y 2 μ)
    (hg : MemLp g 2 μ)
    (hgX : XMeasurable μ X g) :
    ∫ ω, (Y ω - condExpOn μ Y X ω) ^ 2 ∂μ ≤ ∫ ω, (Y ω - g ω) ^ 2 ∂μ := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Unfold the public wrappers condExpOn, XMeasurable, and conditioningSpace, then use the backend theorem for sigma(X).">  simpa [condExpOn, XMeasurable, conditioningSpace] using</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Apply the backend best-predictor theorem with m = sigma(X).">    integral_sq_sub_condExp_le_integral_sq_sub_X
      (m₀ := inferInstance)
      (μ := μ)
      (Y := Y)
      (g := g)
      (X := X)
      hX
      hY
      hg
      hgX</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

The substantial backend proof is
[`integral_sq_sub_condExp_le_integral_sq_sub`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2CondExp.lean#L175):
it expands $(Y-g)^2$, uses
[`integral_mul_cefError_zero`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2CondExp.lean#L114)
to kill the cross term, and uses nonnegativity of a square to finish.

## Downstream uses

- The theorem is the conceptual foundation for Chapter 2's conditional mean
  interpretation.
- The same orthogonality logic reappears in the linear-projection and OLS
  normal-equation arguments in Chapters 2 and 3.
